summary: Market sentiment has turned cautiously optimistic as stocks and bonds rallied following multiple reports that the United States and Iran are closing in on a one-page memorandum to end their conflict, with equities gaining and oil prices sliding on the peace deal hopes. The dominant macro driver is the US-Iran war dynamic, which had previously paused global central bank easing cycles in April, but the prospect of a negotiated resolution — confirmed by Pakistani and Axios sources — is now the primary catalyst reshaping risk appetite across asset classes. Gold climbed to a more than one-week high on the deal hopes, Indian shares advanced, thermal coal received a modest boost from the conflict premium, and Berkshire-owned logistics operations moved to deploy autonomous big rigs across the US Sun Belt, signaling corporate confidence in near-term stability. Key risks investors are monitoring include the fragility of the Iran negotiations given the simultaneous CMA CGM vessel attack in the Strait of Hormuz, China's invocation of its anti-sanctions law to counter US blacklisting of refiners, and broader geopolitical contagion including the US closure of its Peshawar consulate. The short-term outlook hinges almost entirely on whether the one-page Iran memo materializes into a durable ceasefire, as a breakdown would reignite oil price spikes, reassert pressure on central bank timelines, and reverse the current risk-on positioning across equities and bonds.
